messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following ride-hailing app privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"RideNow collects your name, phone number, payment details, and real-time GPS location to provide ride-booking services. Trip history is retained for 2 years. We share your location data with drivers to complete your booking. Your data may be accessed by our teams in the US and India for customer support. You can request data deletion via the app. We use industry-standard encryption."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [FAIL] No lawful basis stated. Ride booking implies Art. 6(1)(b) (contract performance) but this is not named.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PASS] Not applicable. Data collected does not constitute special category data.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Ride booking is a clear and specific purpose. Location sharing with drivers is disclosed as a necessary component.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Retention of detailed trip history for 2 years may be disproportionate — justification not provided.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Trip history retention of 2 years is stated. Retention for payment and account data is not addressed.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Only erasure (data deletion) referenced. Other rights not stated.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PARTIAL] Drivers named as recipients. No mention of payment processor or other sub-processors.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] US and India access disclosed but no transfer safeguard mechanism stated. India does not have an EU adequacy decision.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not addressed. Large-scale location data processing likely requires a DPO.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (lawful basis not stated), Art. 5 (trip history retention not justified), Art. 15-21 (most rights absent), Art. 28 (payment processor not disclosed), Art. 46 (US and India transfer safeguards absent), Art. 37 (DPO likely required), Art. 33 (no breach notification)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- State Art. 6(1)(b) as lawful basis for ride booking
- Justify 2-year trip history retention or reduce it
- Add retention periods for payment and account data
- Add full data subject rights section
- Disclose payment processor and other sub-processors
- Reference transfer safeguards for US and India (SCCs)
- Appoint and disclose a DPO
- Add breach notification commitment
